main_summary.summary: Marilyn Luper Hildreth, daughter of Oklahoma City sit-in leader Clara Luper, was interviewed in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, by Joe Mosnier of the University of North Carolina's Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project. Hildreth recounts her mother's path from Hoffman, Oklahoma, to teaching at Dunjee High School in Spencer, where Clara Luper wrote a play titled Martin Luther King Jr that brought NAACP organizer Herbert L. Wright to Oklahoma City and led to the 1957 NAACP Youth Council tour through the Northern and Southern routes. Hildreth describes the August 19, 1958, Katz Drug Store sit-in at NE 14th Street, which she joined at age eight as one of the thirteen original participants, and the subsequent campaign that desegregated Oklahoma City lunch counters. She recalls residential segregation south of NE 7th Street, segregated water fountains and telephone booths, and her grandmother's work as a maid in white households to educate Clara Luper. Hildreth covers Clara Luper's 26 arrests, her instruction in Mahatma Gandhi's nonviolent methods, the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers strike, Clara Luper's 1972 Oklahoma Democratic US Senate primary campaign, and the white pickup truck incident that produced the one moment Hildreth saw fear in her mother.
key_themes: Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-in (August 19, 1958)
NAACP Youth Council and the 1957 Northern/Southern route tour
Clara Luper's nonviolent pedagogy and Gandhian methods
Residential and public-accommodations segregation in Oklahoma City
Clara Luper's 1972 US Senate primary campaign
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hildreth is the only living daughter-witness of Clara Luper's sit-in leadership and a participant herself in the 1958 Katz Drug Store action that predated Greensboro by sixteen months. Her testimony provides first-person corroboration of the canonical thirteen original sit-in participants, the August 19, 1958, date, the NE 14th Street Katz location, and Clara Luper's tactical strategy of pairing nonviolent training with negotiation across Oklahoma City restaurants.

The interview also preserves under-documented family-history material: Clara Luper's father Ezell Shepard denied hospital treatment because he was Black, Clara Luper's reading of the Congressional Record as a study habit, her electrician husband Calvin Luper Sr.'s difficulty managing the public pressure on the family, and the white pickup truck stalking incident as the single occasion Hildreth saw her mother face fear. These episodes do not appear in mainstream Clara Luper biographical sources and constitute primary-source additions to the Movement-family record.

chapters.title: Project Introduction and Welcome
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier of the University of North Carolina's Southern Oral History Program and filmmaker John Bishop open the interview in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, identifying the project as the joint Library of Congress / Smithsonian NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project. Hildreth is introduced and welcomes the team.
related_events: Library of Congress / Smithsonian NMAAHC partnership

chapters.title: Dunjee High School and the Martin Luther King Jr Play
chapters.summary: Hildreth describes her mother Clara Luper's teaching position at Dunjee High School in Spencer, Oklahoma, and the play Clara Luper wrote titled Martin Luther King Jr. Herbert L. Wright of the NAACP attended a performance and invited the Dunjee students to perform the play at the NAACP National Convention.
related_events: Clara Luper's Martin Luther King Jr play

chapters.title: Segregated Oklahoma City
chapters.summary: Hildreth itemizes the segregation conditions her family lived under in pre-sit-in Oklahoma City: residency restricted south of NE 7th Street, no trying on hats or shoes in stores, separate telephone booths and water fountains, back-door restaurant service via brown bags, and her grandmother tying knots in string to size shoes she could not let Hildreth try on.

chapters.title: The 1957 NAACP Northern and Southern Route Tour
chapters.summary: After Clara Luper's decision to proceed with the NAACP tour, the Youth Council traveled the Northern route to New York City, where the young people drank Cokes in restaurants, ate hamburgers, and used the same water fountains as white patrons for the first time. They returned via the Southern route and re-encountered Jim Crow, which Hildreth says gave them the resolve to begin negotiating with Oklahoma City restaurants.
related_events: 1957 NAACP Youth Council tour to New York City

chapters.title: The Katz Drug Store Sit-In
chapters.summary: At a Youth Council meeting at the Luper home at 1819 NE Park Place, eight-year-old Hildreth made the motion to sit at Katz Drug Store until served. The thirteen original participants were spit on, kicked, and verbally abused but persisted, and segregation walls began falling across Oklahoma City restaurants in quick succession.

chapters.title: Clara Luper as Woman Leader
chapters.summary: Hildreth reflects on her mother's leadership in a male-dominated movement hierarchy. Clara Luper came from a reading-and-education-centered family, gave her children books rather than toys at Christmas, and faced attempts to fire her from the Oklahoma City school system. A teacher was offered the Dunjee principalship if he would fire Clara Luper; he refused.
related_events: Attempted firing of Clara Luper from OKC schools

chapters.title: Hoffman Origins and the Pickup Truck Incident
chapters.summary: Hildreth describes Clara Luper's origins in Hoffman, Oklahoma, where her segregated school used leftover textbooks and her class had five students. The chapter culminates in the white pickup truck stalking incident: returning from a freedom rally in a caravan, Clara Luper directed Hildreth and Barbara Posey Jones to run for the Posey home and drove away alone to lead the truck off. Hildreth identifies this as the only time she saw her mother face fear.
related_events: Clara Luper's childhood in Hoffman, Oklahoma

chapters.title: Fifth Street Baptist and the Black Pastor Network
chapters.summary: Hildreth identifies the Luper family church and discusses Clara Luper's mixed reception among Oklahoma City black pastors: some loved her, some feared her, some opposed her. Many congregations refused to host meetings, fearing their churches would be bombed. Hildreth notes the movement was carried by maids, garbage workers, and have-nots rather than professional Black Oklahomans, who feared losing their jobs.
related_events: Oklahoma City black church politics during the sit-in campaign

chapters.title: Police Relations and the Chimpanzee Incident
chapters.summary: Hildreth describes Oklahoma City's relatively low-violence civil rights climate, attributing it to a conscious local decision to avoid becoming a national flashpoint. Clara Luper was arrested 26 times but not beaten. Hildreth then recounts an incident in which a white opponent threw a chimpanzee on her during a picket; she was small and the chimpanzee was nearly her size.

chapters.title: Clara Luper's Political Savvy and Calvin Luper Sr.
chapters.summary: Hildreth attributes Clara Luper's political effectiveness to her habit of reading the Congressional Record daily, her network across Oklahoma, and her role as the voice of people in struggle. Hildreth then discusses her father Calvin Luper Sr., an electrician whom her parents divorced at an early age; she describes him as fun and loving but says he could not handle the public pressure on the family.

chapters.title: Saturdays of Protest and the 50th Anniversary
chapters.summary: Hildreth reflects on a childhood in which every Saturday was spent protesting, with no time or money for amusement parks. She describes the 50th-anniversary commemoration of the August 19, 1958, Katz sit-in as a standing-room reunion of young people from across the United States, framing the Oklahoma City sit-in as the catalyst that triggered the broader student sit-in wave.
related_events: 50th-anniversary commemoration of the August 19, 1958 OKC Katz sit-in

chapters.title: 1972 US Senate Primary Campaign
chapters.summary: Hildreth recalls her mother's 1972 Oklahoma Democratic US Senate primary campaign as both exhausting and enjoyable. Clara Luper campaigned in small towns with no Black residents and delivered the same message everywhere, drawing cross-racial support. The campaign lost the primary but Clara Luper accepted the outcome as having tried.

chapters.title: 1969 Sanitation Workers Strike and Closing
chapters.summary: Hildreth describes Clara Luper's role as spokesperson for the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers strike, selected by the workers themselves for her integrity and willingness to fight to the end. Hildreth closes with her mother's daily morning question whether she wanted to continue her education that day, and Clara Luper's maxim to believe in the rain when it does not fall, the sun when it does not shine, and a God you have never seen.

loc_video.caption: Marilyn Luper Hildreth oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, 2011 May 24
